what: 1. Setup: fitting Poisson and Negative Binomial models
title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: use irregular_migration dataset shipped package. Two models fitted—Poisson Negative Binomial—year sex covariates alpha year covariates beta. Quick summaries:

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: compare_models() produces side--side table log-likelihood, AIC, BIC, deviance, Pearson chi-squared, RMSE, three pseudo R2R^2 measures: R2_cor: cor(m,μ̂)2\mathrm{cor}(m, \hat\mu)^2 — squared correlation observed fitted values. R2_D: Explained deviance 1−D(model)/D(null)1 - D(\text{model}) / D(\text{null}), null model specification without covariates (single α\alpha, single β\beta). Measures much covariates improve fit beyond baseline power-law structure. R2_CW: Cameron–Windmeijer (1996) pseudo R2R^2, uses model-implied variance function V(μ)V(\mu) — specifically designed count data regression. Models sorted AIC (default). Note R2_D compares fitted model null model (method, covariates). model cov_alpha / cov_beta, R2_D = 0 construction — becomes informative covariates present. Lower AIC BIC values indicate better trade-fit complexity. Pearson chi-squared statistic divided degrees freedom (approximately n_obs - n_par) gives quick dispersion check: values much greater 1 suggest overdispersion, favours NB model Poisson.
code: comp <- compare_models(Poisson = fit_po, NB = fit_nb, sort_by = "AIC") comp #> Model comparison #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>    Model  Method Constrained n_par   logLik      AIC      BIC Deviance #>       NB      NB       FALSE    15 -2621.12  5272.23  5350.70  1275.94 #>  Poisson POISSON       FALSE    14 -8217.25 16462.49 16535.73 13831.69 #>  Pearson_X2   RMSE R2_cor    R2_D  R2_CW #>     3089.52 206.06 0.4054 -0.0029 0.9526 #>    17496.68  52.53 0.8280  0.3139 0.9827

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Poisson model nested within Negative Binomial (Poisson NB theta -> infinity), can use likelihood ratio test. lrtest() applies boundary correction Self & Liang (1987) automatically comparing Poisson NB, dispersion parameter lies boundary parameter space H0. small p-value indicates significant overdispersion, favouring NB model.
code: lrtest(fit_po, fit_nb) #> Likelihood ratio test #> ----------------------------------------  #> Model 1: POISSON   (logLik = -8217.25 ) #> Model 2: NB   (logLik = -2621.12 ) #> LR statistic: 11192.26 on 1 df #> (Boundary-corrected: 0.5 * P(chi2 > LR), Self & Liang 1987) #> p-value: < 2.2e-16

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: residuals() method supports four types: response: raw residuals, mi−μ̂im_i - \hat\mu_i. pearson: standardised variance function, (mi−μ̂)/V(μ̂)(m_i - \hat\mu_i) / \sqrt{V(\hat\mu_i)}. deviance: signed square root individual deviance contributions. anscombe: variance-stabilising residuals (approximately standard normal well-specified model).
code: r_resp <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "response") r_pear <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "pearson") r_dev  <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "deviance") r_ansc <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "anscombe")  summary(r_pear) #>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.  #> -1.15133 -0.59480 -0.28881  0.03623  0.11110 22.90671 summary(r_ansc) #>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.  #> -24.1696  -1.0757  -0.4677  -0.3348   0.1766   9.4208

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: plot() fitted model produces Zhang (2008) four-panel display: Fitted vs Observed (sqrt scale) – points cluster around 45-degree line. Anscombe residuals vs fitted – running mean flat near zero (systematic misfit). Scale-Location – absolute Anscombe residuals vs fitted; increasing trend signals -modelled variance. Normal Q-Q – Anscombe residuals follow reference line distributional assumption holds.  look : Curvature Panel 1 suggests mean function misspecified. non-flat smoother Panel 2 indicates systematic bias certain fitted-value ranges. fan shape Panel 3 points overdispersion heteroscedasticity. Heavy tails S-shapes Panel 4 indicate departure assumed distribution. Compare Poisson fit see whether switching NB resolves patterns:
code: op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(fit_nb, ask = FALSE) par(op) op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(fit_po, ask = FALSE) par(op)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Rootograms (Kleiber & Zeileis, 2016) compare observed fitted count-frequency distributions directly. Three display styles available: hanging (default): observed bars hung fitted curve. model fits well, bottom bar touches zero. standing: observed bars stand x-axis fitted curve overlaid. Harder judge discrepancies visually. suspended: plots difference fobs−fexp\sqrt{f_\text{obs}} - \sqrt{f_\text{exp}} directly. Bars crossing zero indicate - -prediction count.   Compare two: Poisson rootogram shows bars consistently hanging zero line small counts moderate counts, classic overdispersion signature NB model correct.
code: rootogram(fit_nb, style = "hanging") rootogram(fit_po, style = "hanging") rootogram(fit_nb, style = "standing") rootogram(fit_nb, style = "suspended")

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: fitting, plot_explore() produces two log-log scatter plots reveal marginal relationships underlying power-law model: log(m/N) vs log(N): slope approximates α−1\alpha - 1. slope near zero means apprehension rate roughly independent population size (α≈1\alpha \approx 1). negative slope indicates decreasing returns scale (α<1\alpha < 1). log(m/N) vs log(n/N): slope approximates β\beta. positive slope confirms auxiliary rate predictive apprehension rate. Observations m=0m = 0 n=0n = 0 excluded (undefined log scale).  plots exploratory visual checks. OLS lines overlaid rough guides—formal model accounts covariates gamma offset.
code: plot_explore(fit_nb)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: LOO refits model repeatedly, dropping one observation (one country) time, assess much unit influences total population-size estimate.

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Dropping rows country measures structural contribution. computationally intensive (one refit per country). print() method shows top 10 influential countries ranked |Δξ||\Delta\xi|, summary() reports coefficient xi stability across LOO refits. xi bar plot shows countries pull total estimate (red bars, dropping country decreases estimate) (blue bars).
code: loo_po_ctry <- loo(fit_po, by = "country", verbose = TRUE) loo_nb_ctry <- loo(fit_nb, by = "country", verbose = TRUE) print(loo_nb_ctry) summary(loo_nb_ctry) plot(loo_nb_ctry, type = "xi") plot(loo_nb_ctry, type = "coef")

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Dropping individual rows identifies specific data points drive estimate. Useful outlier detection.
code: loo_nb_obs <- loo(fit_nb, by = "obs", verbose = TRUE) print(loo_nb_obs) plot(loo_nb_obs)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: compare_loo() brings together LOO results two models produces scatter plot axis shows percentage change xi dropping given unit: Scatter plot interpretation (4 quadrants): Top-right (+, +): dropping unit increases estimate models. unit pulling estimate . Bottom-left (-, -): dropping unit decreases estimate models. unit pulling estimate . -diagonal (top-left bottom-right): divergent influence. unit affects two models opposite directions—sign model choice matters unit. Near origin: non-influential models. Points near diagonal indicate consistent influence; points far diagonal warrant investigation. side--side bar plot ranks countries maximum absolute percentage change across models, making easy spot consequential units.
code: comp_loo <- compare_loo(   loo_po_ctry, loo_nb_ctry,   labels = c("Poisson", "NB") ) print(comp_loo) plot(comp_loo, type = "scatter") plot(comp_loo, type = "bar")

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Estimating size unauthorized (hidden) populations fundamental problem official statistics migration research. definition, populations avoid administrative registration, single data source provides complete count. However, multiple partial administrative sources available – example, border-guard apprehensions, police identifications, social-insurance records – statistical models can exploit cross-source variation infer size population escapes sources. uncounted package implements estimation framework Beresewicz, Gudaszewski Walsh (2025), builds dual-system ideas Zhang (2008). core insight observed count unauthorized migrants given group (e.g., country--origin ×\times year cell) power-law function reference (known) population, modulated auxiliary registration rate. Fitting power-law model inverting yields estimate total hidden population. vignette describes theory behind model, available estimation methods, inference procedures implemented package.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Let =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n index observational units (e.g., country--origin ×\times year ×\times sex cells). Denote mim_i: observed count unauthorized migrants (e.g., apprehensions), NiN_i: reference population size (e.g., insured foreigners origin), nin_i: auxiliary count second administrative source (e.g., police identifications). conditional mean mim_i modelled E[mi∣Ni,ni]=Niα(ni+γNi)β, E[m_i \mid N_i, n_i] \;=\; N_i^{\alpha}\,   \left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right)^{\!\beta}, α\alpha, β\beta, γ≥0\gamma \geq 0 parameters estimated. log scale, becomes linear model: logE[mi]=αlogNi+βlog(ni+γNi). \log E[m_i] \;=\;   \alpha \,\log N_i \;+\;   \beta \,\log\!\left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right). Note argument second logarithm can rewritten γ+ni/Ni\gamma + n_i / N_i ratio form used inside code.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: α\alpha – elasticity respect reference population. parameter α\alpha governs expected observed count scales reference population. α=1\alpha = 1, relationship proportional; α∈(0,1)\alpha \(0, 1), scaling sublinear, meaning larger reference populations yield proportionally fewer detections. Values α\alpha near zero indicate weak dependence population size. constraint α∈(0,1)\alpha \(0, 1) theoretically motivated always imposed estimation (see Section “Covariates” ). β\beta – elasticity respect registration rate. parameter β\beta captures association auxiliary registration rate (γ+ni/Ni)(\gamma + n_i / N_i) observed unauthorized count. positive β\beta indicates groups higher auxiliary detection rates also tend observed unauthorized migrants. expected sources partially detect latent population. γ\gamma – offset zero auxiliary counts. Many groups may ni=0n_i = 0 (auxiliary detections), make log(ni/Ni)\log(n_i / N_i) undefined. offset γ≥0\gamma \geq 0 ensures rate term remains positive. practice, γ\gamma typically small (close zero). can fixed known value, estimated data, omitted entirely ni>0n_i > 0.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Given α̂\hat\alpha, total unauthorized population estimated ξ̂=∑=1nNiα̂. \hat\xi \;=\; \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\hat\alpha}. α̂<1\hat\alpha < 1 Ni>1N_i > 1 ii, ξ̂<∑iNi\hat\xi < \sum_i N_i, reflecting fact fraction reference population unauthorized. population size ξ̂\hat\xi monotone increasing function α\alpha (Ni≥1N_i \geq 1), property exploited confidence interval construction.
code: library(uncounted) data(irregular_migration)  ## Basic Poisson model with estimated gamma fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data      = irregular_migration,   observed  = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method    = "poisson" ) summary(fit)  ## Population size estimate with 95% CI popsize(fit)

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: scalar parameters α\alpha β\beta can made group-specific design matrices. Let 𝐗α,\mathbf{X}_{\alpha,} 𝐗β,\mathbf{X}_{\beta,} covariate row vectors observation ii. unconstrained specification αi=𝐗α,⊤𝐚,βi=𝐗β,⊤𝐛, \alpha_i = \mathbf{X}_{\alpha,}^\top \mathbf{}, \qquad \beta_i  = \mathbf{X}_{\beta,}^\top \mathbf{b}, 𝐚\mathbf{} 𝐛\mathbf{b} coefficient vectors.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: unconstrained case, nothing prevents α̂\hat\alpha_i falling outside (0,1)(0, 1) β̂\hat\beta_i negative. theoretical constraints desired, link functions can applied: αi=logit−1(𝐗α,⊤𝐚)=11+exp(−𝐗α,⊤𝐚),βi=exp(𝐗β,⊤𝐛), \alpha_i = \mathrm{logit^{-1}}(\mathbf{X}_{\alpha,}^\top \mathbf{})   = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\mathbf{X}_{\alpha,}^\top \mathbf{})},   \qquad \beta_i = \exp(\mathbf{X}_{\beta,}^\top \mathbf{b}), ensuring αi∈(0,1)\alpha_i \(0, 1) βi>0\beta_i > 0. Constrained estimation available Poisson Negative Binomial methods (set constrained = TRUE). Coefficients reported link scale; response-scale values shown summary().
code: ## Alpha and beta varying by sex fit_cov <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data      = irregular_migration,   observed  = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method    = "poisson",   cov_alpha = ~ sex,   cov_beta  = ~ sex ) summary(fit_cov)  ## Constrained Poisson: alpha in (0,1), beta > 0 fit_constr <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data      = irregular_migration,   observed  = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method    = "poisson",   cov_alpha = ~ sex,   constrained = TRUE ) summary(fit_constr)

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: uncounted package implements four estimation methods. share mean structure; differ objective function distributional assumptions.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Ordinary least squares applied log-linearized model: logmi=αlogNi+βlog(ni+γNi)+εi. \log m_i = \alpha \log N_i + \beta \log\!\left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right) + \varepsilon_i. mi=0m_i = 0, response replaced log(mi+1)\log(m_i + 1). OLS fast transparent ignores count nature mim_i may biased heteroscedasticity (log-transformation changes conditional mean). γ\gamma estimated, profiled via grid search: candidate γ\gamma, OLS coefficients obtained closed form, value minimizing residual sum squares selected.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Nonlinear least squares minimizes ∑=1n(mi−Niα(ni+γNi)β)2 \sum_{=1}^n \left(m_i - N_i^{\alpha}   \left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right)^{\!\beta}\right)^2 using Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm. avoids log-transformation bias OLS still treats mim_i continuous.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Poisson log-likelihood ℓ(𝛉)=∑=1n[milogμi−μi], \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{=1}^{n}   \bigl[m_i \log \mu_i - \mu_i\bigr], μi=Niαi(γ+ni/Ni)βi\mu_i = N_i^{\alpha_i}\,(\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta_i}. shown Santos Silva Tenreyro (2006), Poisson PML estimator consistent parameters conditional mean E[mi∣Ni,ni]E[m_i \mid N_i, n_i] even true distribution Poisson, provided mean correctly specified. makes PPML robust heteroscedasticity recommended default uncounted.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Negative Binomial extends Poisson adding dispersion parameter θ>0\theta > 0. log-likelihood $$ \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \theta) = \sum_{=1}^{n} \Bigl[   \log\Gamma(m_i + \theta) - \log\Gamma(\theta) - \log(m_i!)   + \theta\log\theta - \theta\log(\theta + \mu_i)   + m_i\log\mu_i - m_i\log(\theta + \mu_i) \Bigr]. $$ accounts overdispersion (variance exceeding Poisson mean) can provide efficient estimates data truly overdispersed. parameter θ\theta jointly estimated alongside α\alpha, β\beta, γ\gamma.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: offset parameter γ\gamma handled differently depending method: Poisson / NB: γ\gamma jointly optimized within likelihood using L-BFGS-B box constraints (gamma_bounds, default [10−10,0.5][10^{-10},\, 0.5]). OLS: γ\gamma profiled via grid search bounds, followed local optimization (optimize()). methods: γ\gamma can fixed known value (gamma = 0.01) omitted entirely (gamma = NULL), latter requiring ni>0n_i > 0 observations.
code: ## Compare estimation methods fit_ols <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = irregular_migration,   observed = ~ m, auxiliary = ~ n, reference_pop = ~ N,   method = "ols" )  fit_pois <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = irregular_migration,   observed = ~ m, auxiliary = ~ n, reference_pop = ~ N,   method = "poisson" )  fit_nb <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = irregular_migration,   observed = ~ m, auxiliary = ~ n, reference_pop = ~ N,   method = "nb" )  ## Extract population size estimates popsize(fit_ols) popsize(fit_pois) popsize(fit_nb)

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: estimation methods report heteroscedasticity-consistent (HC) robust standard errors computed via sandwich estimator. general form Var̂(𝛉̂)=(𝐉⊤𝐉)−1𝐉⊤diag(ẽ12,…,ẽn2)𝐉(𝐉⊤𝐉)−1, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) =   (\mathbf{J}^\top \mathbf{J})^{-1}\,   \mathbf{J}^\top \,\mathrm{diag}(\tilde{e}_1^2, \ldots, \tilde{e}_n^2)\,   \mathbf{J}\,   (\mathbf{J}^\top \mathbf{J})^{-1}, 𝐉\mathbf{J} Jacobian (score matrix) ẽ\tilde{e}_i adjusted residuals. adjustment depends HC variant: hiih_{ii} hat-matrix diagonal elements (leverages) δi\delta_i data-dependent exponent. HC3 (default) recommended moderate sample sizes provides good balance finite-sample bias correction stability.

what: Delta-method confidence intervals for population size
title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: population size ξ̂=∑iNiα̂\hat\xi = \sum_i N_i^{\hat\alpha} nonlinear function α̂\hat\alpha. Confidence intervals constructed via monotone transformation Wald interval link scale. Step 1. Build Wald interval linear predictor η̂\hat\eta (equals α̂\hat\alpha unconstrained, logit α̂\hat\alpha constrained): η̂±zα/2⋅se(η̂), \hat\eta \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \mathrm{se}(\hat\eta), se(η̂)=𝐱⊤𝐕𝐱\mathrm{se}(\hat\eta) = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{V} \mathbf{x}} uses HC-robust covariance 𝐕\mathbf{V}. Step 2. Map interval endpoints monotone function g(α)=∑iNiαg(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^\alpha: ξ̂L=∑iNiαL,ξ̂U=∑iNiαU. \hat\xi_L = \sum_i N_i^{\alpha_L}, \qquad \hat\xi_U = \sum_i N_i^{\alpha_U}. Since gg monotone increasing Ni≥1N_i \geq 1, resulting interval [ξ̂L,ξ̂U][\hat\xi_L, \hat\xi_U] correct coverage probability (asymptotically). Total across groups. model includes covariates α\alpha, total ξ̂=∑gξ̂g\hat\xi = \sum_g \hat\xi_g delta-method CI computed via gradient ∇αξ\nabla_\alpha \xi log-normal approximation ensure positivity.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: ξ(α)=∑iNiα\xi(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^\alpha convex α\alpha Ni>1N_i > 1, Jensen’s inequality implies E[ξ̂]≥ξE[\hat\xi] \geq \xi. second-order Taylor expansion h(α)=∑iNiαh(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^\alpha around true α\alpha gives approximate bias: Bias(ξ̂)≈12∑=1nNiα(logNi)2⋅𝐱⊤𝐕model𝐱, \mathrm{Bias}(\hat\xi) \;\approx\;   \frac{1}{2}\,\sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\alpha}\,(\log N_i)^2 \;\cdot\;   \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{model}}\,\mathbf{x}, 𝐕model\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{model}} model-based (homoscedastic) variance–covariance matrix. bias-corrected estimator ξ̂BC=ξ̂−Biaŝ. \hat\xi^{BC} = \hat\xi - \widehat{\mathrm{Bias}}. Model-based variance used rather HC-robust variance, latter can inflated high-leverage observations skewed data, leading overcorrection. Bias correction also applied CI bounds, evaluated respective α\alpha values.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: finite-sample inference robustness check analytical CIs, package provides fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB) Xu et al. (2020) via bootstrap_popsize(). Instead resampling rows, FWB generates random non-negative weights w1,…,wnw_1, \ldots, w_n Dirichlet(1,…,1)\mathrm{Dirichlet}(1, \ldots, 1) distribution refits model weights. avoids duplicate-row issues discrete data naturally suited weighted GLMs. Cluster bootstrap. observations independent (e.g., country observed across years sexes), cluster variable can specified. case, observations within cluster receive FWB weight, preserving within-cluster correlation. Bootstrap CIs. Two types available: Percentile: α/2\alpha/2 (1−α/2)(1 - \alpha/2) quantiles bootstrap distribution. Bias-corrected percentile (BC): adjusts quantile probabilities median bias using standard BC formula. bootstrap median default point estimate transformation ξ=∑Niα\xi = \sum N_i^\alpha convex, bootstrap distribution ξ̂\hat\xi right-skewed bootstrap mean tends overestimate ξ\xi.
code: ## Fit a model with country-level grouping fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data      = irregular_migration,   observed  = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method    = "poisson",   countries = ~ country )  ## Cluster bootstrap with 199 replicates boot_result <- bootstrap_popsize(   fit,   R       = 199,   cluster = ~ country_code,   seed    = 42 ) boot_result  ## Access the full results table boot_result$popsize_full

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: vignette walks complete analysis estimating unauthorized foreign population Poland using irregular_migration dataset shipped uncounted package. workflow covers every step loading data fitting models, obtaining population size estimates confidence intervals, running bootstrap inference, selecting among competing specifications, performing sensitivity analysis. end reproducible pipeline can adapted datasets.
code: library(uncounted)

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: irregular_migration dataset country-level panel (2019–2024) three count variables per observation: m – foreigners apprehended Border Guard unauthorized stay (quantity whose hidden population want estimate). n – foreigners identified Police (auxiliary, partially overlapping administrative source). N – foreigners registered Social Insurance Institution (ZUS), used proxy total known foreign population given country. large share observations zero apprehensions, reflecting many small countries origin recorded unauthorized stay. log–log scatter apprehension count reference population reveals power-law relationship motivates model. Observations m > 0 n > 0 shown.

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: fit four specifications, using formula interface. core model E(mi)=Niα(γ+ni/Ni)β,E(m_i) = N_i^{\alpha}\,(\gamma + n_i / N_i)^{\beta}, α\alpha governs elasticity respect reference population, β\beta captures relationship auxiliary detection rate, γ\gamma baseline offset ensuring rate term positive even ni=0n_i = 0.

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: recommended default. Poisson pseudo-maximum likelihood (PPML) consistent conditional mean even heteroscedasticity, gamma estimated jointly. Key output look : alpha – elasticity respect N. Values 1 indicate unauthorized population grows less proportionally reference population. beta – elasticity respect detection rate. Positive values mean higher auxiliary rates associated apprehensions. gamma – estimated baseline offset (typically small).
code: fit_pois <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = irregular_migration,   observed = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method = "poisson",   gamma = "estimate",   countries = ~ country ) summary(fit_pois) #> Unauthorized population estimation #> Method: POISSON | vcov: HC3  #> N obs: 1382  #> Gamma: 0.001831 (estimated)  #> Log-likelihood: -11380.65  #> AIC: 22767.3  BIC: 22782.99  #> Deviance: 20158.5  #>  #> Coefficients: #>       Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)     #> alpha 0.736646   0.040258  18.298 < 2.2e-16 *** #> beta  0.575046   0.091861   6.260  3.85e-10 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> ----------------------- #> Population size estimation results: #>   (BC = bias-corrected using model-based variance) #>       Observed Estimate Estimate (BC) CI lower CI upper #> (all)   27,105  290,597       290,498  127,726  660,703

what: Model 4: NB two-stage (gamma from Poisson, fixed in NB)
title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: pragmatic two-stage approach: first estimate gamma Poisson model (better identified), fix NB model. avoids potential numerical instability estimating gamma theta simultaneously.
code: gamma_from_pois <- fit_pois$gamma  fit_nb2 <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = irregular_migration,   observed = ~ m,   auxiliary = ~ n,   reference_pop = ~ N,   method = "nb",   gamma = gamma_from_pois,   countries = ~ country ) summary(fit_nb2) #> Unauthorized population estimation #> Method: NB | vcov: HC1  #> N obs: 1382  #> Gamma: 0.001831 (fixed)  #> Theta (NB dispersion): 0.9491  #> Log-likelihood: -2727.07  #> AIC: 5460.14  BIC: 5475.84  #> Deviance: 1267.27  #>  #> Coefficients: #>       Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)     #> alpha 0.745560   0.015958  46.721 < 2.2e-16 *** #> beta  0.597130   0.023991  24.890 < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> ----------------------- #> Population size estimation results: #>   (BC = bias-corrected using model-based variance) #>       Observed Estimate Estimate (BC) CI lower CI upper #> (all)   27,105  318,958       315,391  227,252  437,716

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: popsize() function extracts estimated total unauthorized population ξ̂=∑iNiα̂\hat{\xi} = \sum_i N_i^{\hat{\alpha}} along bias-corrected estimates confidence intervals. columns : group – label alpha-covariate group ("()" since covariates alpha). observed – total observed count ∑mi\sum m_i. estimate – plug-estimate ξ̂\hat{\xi}. estimate_bc – bias-corrected estimate using second-order Taylor expansion. ξ(α)=∑Niα\xi(\alpha) = \sum N_i^\alpha convex, plug-estimator biased upward Jensen’s inequality. lower, upper – confidence interval bounds. CI constructed monotone transformation Wald interval link scale, bias correction applied bounds. share_pct – group share percentage total. Comparing across four models:
code: ps_pois <- popsize(fit_pois) ps_pois #>   group observed estimate estimate_bc    lower    upper share_pct #> 1 (all)    27105 290597.5    290498.3 127726.4 660703.2       100 models <- list(   Poisson       = fit_pois,   Poisson_constr = fit_pois_c,   NB            = fit_nb,   NB_twostage   = fit_nb2 )  ps_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(models), function(nm) {   ps <- popsize(models[[nm]])   data.frame(     Model    = nm,     Estimate = sum(ps$estimate),     BC       = sum(ps$estimate_bc),     Lower    = sum(ps$lower),     Upper    = sum(ps$upper)   ) })) ps_table #>            Model  Estimate        BC    Lower     Upper #> 1        Poisson  290597.5  290498.3 127726.4  660703.2 #> 2 Poisson_constr  290597.4  290520.0 127736.0  660752.4 #> 3             NB 1259565.1 1231048.3 731135.6 2072775.5 #> 4    NB_twostage  318957.8  315391.5 227251.8  437716.2

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: bootstrap_popsize() function uses fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB) Xu et al. (2020). Cluster bootstrap country recommended account within-country correlation across years. Access detailed results:
code: set.seed(2025) boot_pois <- bootstrap_popsize(   fit_pois,   R = 999,   cluster = ~ country_code,   ci_type = "perc",   seed = 2025 ) boot_pois # #> Bootstrap population size estimation # #> R = 999 | CI type: perc | Point estimate: median | Converged: 998 / 999 # #> Cluster bootstrap # #> 95% CI # Main table (selected point estimate + CI) boot_pois$popsize  # Full table with all point estimate types boot_pois$popsize_full  # Bootstrap distribution summary summary(boot_pois)

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: bootstrap median typically smaller plug-estimate. expected: ξ(α)=∑Niα\xi(\alpha) = \sum N_i^\alpha convex α\alpha Ni>1N_i > 1, Jensen’s inequality E[ξ̂]≥ξE[\hat{\xi}] \geq \xi. bootstrap mean inherits upward bias, bootstrap median robust right skew bootstrap distribution therefore recommended point estimate.
code: ps_analytical <- popsize(fit_pois) data.frame(   Type       = c("Analytical", "Bootstrap"),   Estimate   = c(sum(ps_analytical$estimate_bc),                  boot_pois$popsize$estimate),   Lower      = c(sum(ps_analytical$lower),                  boot_pois$popsize$lower),   Upper      = c(sum(ps_analytical$upper),                  boot_pois$popsize$upper) )

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: output includes three pseudo R2R^2 measures: R2_cor (squared correlation), R2_D (explained deviance relative null model without covariates, see Cameron & Trivedi, 2013), R2_CW (Cameron–Windmeijer, 1997, accounts model’s variance function). Note R2_D: null model specification (method, gamma) without cov_alpha / cov_beta — .e., single α\alpha single β\beta. model already covariates, model = null R2_D = 0 construction. R2_D becomes informative compare models covariates (e.g., cov_alpha = ~ year + sex) covariate-free baseline: tells much deviance explained group-varying parameters beyond basic power-law structure.
code: comp <- compare_models(   Poisson        = fit_pois,   Poisson_constr = fit_pois_c,   NB             = fit_nb,   NB_twostage    = fit_nb2,   sort_by = "AIC" ) comp #> Model comparison #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>           Model  Method Constrained n_par    logLik      AIC      BIC Deviance #>              NB      NB       FALSE     4  -2693.48  5394.97  5415.89  1272.20 #>     NB_twostage      NB       FALSE     3  -2727.07  5460.14  5475.84  1267.27 #>  Poisson_constr POISSON        TRUE     3 -11380.65 22767.30 22782.99 20158.50 #>         Poisson POISSON       FALSE     3 -11380.65 22767.30 22782.99 20158.50 #>  Pearson_X2   RMSE R2_cor R2_D  R2_CW #>     2943.93 139.85 0.5168    0 0.9432 #>     2600.28  82.27 0.5620    0 0.9442 #>    25366.63  81.47 0.5623    0 0.9749 #>    25366.63  81.47 0.5623    0 0.9749

what: Likelihood ratio test: Poisson vs NB
title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: comparing Poisson (H0) NB (H1), dispersion parameter theta lies boundary parameter space H0 (θ→∞\theta \\infty). function automatically applies Self & Liang (1987) correction, yielding p=0.5⋅Pr(χ12>LR)p = 0.5 \cdot \Pr(\chi^2_1 > LR).
code: lr <- lrtest(fit_pois, fit_nb) lr #> Likelihood ratio test #> ----------------------------------------  #> Model 1: POISSON   (logLik = -11380.65 ) #> Model 2: NB   (logLik = -2693.48 ) #> LR statistic: 17374.33 on 1 df #> (Boundary-corrected: 0.5 * P(chi2 > LR), Self & Liang 1987) #> p-value: < 2.2e-16

title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: four-panel diagnostic plot shows fitted vs observed, Anscombe residuals, scale-location, normal Q-Q plot.  rootogram compares observed fitted count frequencies. Hanging bars touch zero line indicate good fit.
code: par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(fit_pois, ask = FALSE) rootogram(fit_pois) rootogram(fit_nb)

what: Why Poisson as the main model?
title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: Despite NB often better AIC/BIC (reflecting genuine overdispersion), Poisson model recommended primary specification several reasons: Consistency. Poisson PML consistent conditional mean long E(mi|Xi)E(m_i | X_i) correctly specified, regardless true variance function. NB requires correct specification mean variance. Robustness. HC-robust standard errors correct overdispersion Poisson model without adding extra parameter must estimated. Stability. NB model’s joint estimation gamma theta can numerically fragile many observations zero. two-stage approach mitigates introduces conditioning step. NB results serve useful robustness check population size estimate.

what: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: Computes bootstrap confidence intervals estimated population size using fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB) Xu et al. (2020). Supports cluster bootstrap account within-country correlation across years sex.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
code: bootstrap_popsize(   object,   R = 199,   cluster = NULL,   level = 0.95,   ci_type = c("perc", "bc"),   point_estimate = c("median", "plugin", "mean"),   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   by = NULL,   total = FALSE )

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: object "uncounted" object (fitted model). R Number bootstrap replications (default 199). cluster One-sided formula cluster variable (e.g., ~ country_code). provided, FWB weights generated cluster level (observations cluster get weight). NULL, observation-level FWB used. level Confidence level (default 0.95). ci_type Type bootstrap CI: "perc" (percentile) "bc" (bias-corrected percentile). Default "perc". point_estimate point estimate report: "median" (default, recommended) – bootstrap median, robust skewness; "plugin" – plug-\(\hat{\xi} = \sum N^{\hat{\alpha}}\); "mean" – bootstrap mean. seed Random seed reproducibility. verbose Print progress bar? Default TRUE. Optional formula stratified population size estimation (e.g., ~ year, ~ country). provided, bootstrap population sizes computed per -group instead per cov_alpha group. See popsize details. total Logical; TRUE multiple groups exist, include total row computed summing per-replicate group estimates taking quantiles. Default FALSE.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: object class "uncounted_boot" components: t Matrix (R x n_groups): bootstrap population size estimates per group. row one replicate. t0 Numeric vector: plug-point estimates original fit. t0_bc Numeric vector: bias-corrected point estimates (Taylor expansion). popsize Data frame columns: group, estimate, lower, upper, estimate uses chosen point_estimate type. popsize_full Data frame point estimate types: plugin, plugin_bc, boot_median, boot_mean, lower, upper. total List total across groups (plugin, plugin_bc, median, mean, lower, upper), NULL one group. R Number replications requested. ci_type CI type used ("perc" "bc"). point_estimate Point estimate type used. level Confidence level. n_converged Number bootstrap replications model converged. cluster Logical; whether cluster bootstrap used.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: Fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB). Instead resampling rows, FWB generates random non-negative weights Dirichlet distribution refits model weighted likelihood (Xu et al., 2020). avoids duplicate-row issues discrete data better suited GLMs. fwb package provides implementation. Cluster bootstrap. cluster specified, observations within cluster (e.g., country) receive FWB weight. preserves within-cluster correlation structure (e.g., country observed across multiple years sex categories). Cluster bootstrap recommended whenever observations independent. bootstrap median point estimate. transformation \(\xi = \sum N_i^{\alpha}\) convex \(\alpha\) \(N > 1\), Jensen's inequality \(E[\hat{\xi}] \geq \xi\). bootstrap distribution \(\hat{\xi}\) therefore typically right-skewed, bootstrap mean overestimates \(\xi\). bootstrap median robust central-tendency summary less sensitive skewness, default point estimate. Percentile CI (ci_type = "perc"). interval \([\hat{\xi}^{*}_{\alpha/2}, \; \hat{\xi}^{*}_{1-\alpha/2}]\), .e., \(\alpha/2\) \(1-\alpha/2\) quantiles bootstrap distribution. Bias-corrected percentile CI (ci_type = "bc"). Adjusts quantile probabilities median bias. Let \(z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(\mathrm{Pr}(\hat{\xi}^* < \hat{\xi}_0))\). adjusted quantile probabilities \(p_L = \Phi(2z_0 + z_{\alpha/2})\) \(p_U = \Phi(2z_0 - z_{\alpha/2})\), yielding \([\hat{\xi}^{*}_{p_L}, \; \hat{\xi}^{*}_{p_U}]\). Total across groups. multiple groups exist, total computed summing per-replicate group estimates taking quantiles. correctly accounts -group correlation within bootstrap replicate, unlike summing marginal quantiles.

title: Compare LOO Results from Two Models — compare_loo
text: Purpose. comparing two model specifications (e.g., Poisson vs NB, different covariate sets), useful check whether observations influential models, whether influence patterns diverge. Scatter plot interpretation (4 quadrants). scatter plot (type = "scatter") shows \(\%\Delta\xi\) Model 1 (x-axis) vs Model 2 (y-axis). four quadrants reveal: Top-right (+, +): dropping obs increases \(\hat{\xi}\) models (obs pulls estimate ). Bottom-left (-, -): dropping obs decreases \(\hat{\xi}\) models (obs pulls estimate ). Top-left (-, +): divergent influence – obs pulls estimate opposite directions across models. Bottom-right (+, -): top-left reversed. Points near diagonal similar influence models. Points far diagonal divergent influence warrant investigation. Bar plot. bar plot (type = "bar") shows top N influential observations side side models, ranked max(|pct_1|, |pct_2|).

title: Compare multiple uncounted models — compare_models
text: Method Estimation method (OLS, NLS, POISSON, NB). Constrained Whether model fitted parameter constraints. n_par Number estimated parameters. logLik Log-likelihood (true count models, Gaussian pseudo-log-likelihood OLS/NLS). AIC, BIC Akaike Bayesian information criteria. Deviance Model deviance (see deviance.uncounted). Pearson_X2 Pearson chi-squared statistic \(\sum(m_i - \hat\mu_i)^2 / V(\hat\mu_i)\). RMSE Root mean squared error response residuals. R2_cor Squared correlation observed fitted values: \(\mathrm{cor}(m, \hat\mu)^2\). R2_D Explained deviance pseudo \(R^2\): \(1 - D(\text{model}) / D(\text{null})\), null model specification without covariates (single \(\alpha\), single \(\beta\)). Poisson/NB. See Cameron Trivedi (2013). R2_CW Cameron–Windmeijer (1996) pseudo \(R^2\): \(1 - \sum (m_i - \hat\mu_i)^2 / V(\hat\mu_i) \big/     \sum (m_i - \bar m)^2 / V(\bar m)\). Uses model-implied variance function \(V(\mu)\). Poisson/NB. warning issued comparing OLS/NLS pseudo-log-likelihoods true count-model likelihoods, since AIC/BIC values comparable across families.

title: Deviance for uncounted models — deviance.uncounted
text: formula depends estimation method: Poisson: $$D = 2\sum_{}\bigl[m_i\log(m_i/\hat\mu_i) - (m_i - \hat\mu_i)\bigr]$$ convention \(0 \log 0 = 0\). Negative Binomial: $$D = 2\sum_{}\bigl[m_i\log(m_i/\hat\mu_i) -   (m_i+\theta)\log\bigl((m_i+\theta)/(\hat\mu_i+\theta)\bigr)\bigr]$$ OLS / NLS: $$D = \sum_{}(\log m_i - \log\hat\mu_i)^2$$ .e., residual sum squares log scale.

text: object class "uncounted", list containing: coefficients Named vector estimated coefficients. alpha_coefs, beta_coefs Coefficient sub-vectors alpha beta equations. alpha_values, beta_values Per-observation alpha beta response scale (applying link inverse constrained). vcov HC-robust variance-covariance matrix. vcov_model Model-based (homoscedastic) variance-covariance matrix, used bias correction. fitted.values Fitted values \(\hat{m}_i\). residuals Raw residuals \(m_i - \hat{m}_i\). gamma Estimated fixed gamma value (NULL). theta NB dispersion parameter (method = "nb"). loglik Log-likelihood (Poisson NB methods). method estimation method used.

title: Estimate the Size of an Unauthorized Migrant Population — estimate_hidden_pop
text: Model specification. observation \(\), expected observed count \(m_i\) modelled : $$E(m_i) = N_i^{\alpha_i} \cdot (\gamma + n_i / N_i)^{\beta_i}$$ \(N_i\) reference (total registered) population, \(n_i\) auxiliary count (e.g., new registrations), \(\gamma \ge 0\) intercept-like offset. log scale, model linear: $$\log E(m_i) = \alpha_i \log N_i + \beta_i \log(\gamma + n_i / N_i)$$ Parameter interpretation. \(\alpha\) Elasticity observed count respect reference population. \(\alpha < 1\), total (unobserved) population \(\xi = \sum_i N_i^\alpha\) smaller \(\sum_i N_i\), reflecting incomplete coverage. Values \(\alpha\) near 0 imply weak dependence population size; values near 1 imply near-proportional scaling. \(\beta\) Elasticity respect registration rate \(\gamma + n_i / N_i\). positive \(\beta\) means higher auxiliary rates associated observed unauthorized migrants. \(\gamma\) Baseline registration-rate offset, ensuring rate term positive even \(n_i = 0\). Typically small (close zero). gamma = "estimate", profiled (OLS) jointly optimized (MLE methods). Estimation methods. OLS Ordinary least squares log-linearized model. Fast transparent ignores count nature \(m_i\) may inefficient heteroscedasticity. Uses \(\log(m_i)\) response (\(\log(m_i + 1)\) zeros present). Note: \(\log(m_i + 1)\) transformation used, fitted values \(\exp(\hat{\mu})\) \(\hat{\mu}\) estimated shifted scale, response-scale residuals diagnostics approximate. NLS Nonlinear least squares original scale \(m_i = N_i^{\alpha} (\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta} + \varepsilon_i\). Avoids log-transformation bias OLS still treats \(m_i\) continuous. Poisson Poisson pseudo-maximum likelihood (PPML). Consistent heteroscedasticity long conditional mean correctly specified (Santos Silva Tenreyro, 2006). Recommended default. NB Negative Binomial MLE. Adds dispersion parameter \(\theta\) accommodate overdispersion beyond Poisson allows. Standard errors regression coefficients computed conditional \(\hat{\theta}\), consistent approach used MASS::glm.nb. means coefficient SEs account uncertainty \(\theta\) estimation. Constrained vs. unconstrained estimation. constrained = FALSE (default), \(\alpha\) \(\beta\) estimated real line without restrictions. constrained = TRUE, link functions enforce parameter bounds: \(\alpha = \mathrm{logit}(\eta)\) \(\alpha \(0, 1)\), \(\beta = \exp(\zeta)\) \(\beta > 0\). Coefficients reported link (transformed) scale; use summary() alpha_values / beta_values elements returned object response-scale values. Constrained estimation available Poisson NB methods . Population size estimation. total unauthorized population estimated \(\hat{\xi} = \sum_i N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_i}\). See popsize bias correction confidence intervals.

Documents 2008/11, Statistics Norway. https://www.ssb.//english/publikasjoner/pdf/doc_200811_en/doc_200811_en.pdf Beręsewicz, M. Pawlukiewicz, K. (2020). Estimation number irregular foreigners Poland using non-linear count regression models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2008.09407. Santos Silva, J. M. C. Tenreyro, S. (2006). log gravity. Review Economics Statistics, 88(4), 641–658.

title: Leave-One-Out Sensitivity Analysis — loo
text: Purpose. LOO sensitivity analysis assesses much observation (country) influences estimated population size. observation large \(|\Delta\xi|\) relative full-model \(\hat{\xi}\) "influential" may warrant closer inspection. = "obs" vs = "country". = "obs", row dropped individually (n refits). identifies individual data points drive estimate, useful detecting outliers data errors. = "country", rows belonging one country dropped simultaneously (one refit per country). measures country's overall contribution \(\hat{\xi}\) relevant assessing structural sensitivity: conclusion change country excluded? Interpretation dxi. \(\Delta\xi_i = \hat{\xi}_{(-)} - \hat{\xi}\): change total estimated population observation (country) \(\) removed. \(\Delta\xi_i < 0\): dropping \(\) decreases estimate (observation pulling estimate ). \(\Delta\xi_i > 0\): dropping \(\) increases estimate (observation pulling estimate ).

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: object class "uncounted_lrtest" components statistic, df, p_value, boundary, model descriptions, log-likelihoods.

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: $$LR = 2\bigl(\ell_2 - \ell_1\bigr)$$ \(\ell_1\) \(\ell_2\) maximised log-likelihoods simpler complex models, respectively. \(H_0\) statistic follows \(\chi^2\) distribution degrees freedom equal difference number estimated parameters. Boundary correction Poisson vs NB. comparing Poisson model (H0) Negative Binomial model (H1), dispersion parameter \(\theta\) lies boundary parameter space \(H_0\) (\(\theta \\infty\)). standard \(\chi^2\) approximation invalid setting. Following Self & Liang (1987), p-value corrected $$p = 0.5\,\Pr(\chi^2_1 > LR)$$ corresponds 50:50 mixture point mass zero \(\chi^2_1\). function intended comparing count models (Poisson NB). OLS/NLS models supported use pseudo-log-likelihood. models must fitted data (identical number observations).

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: Self, S. G. Liang, K.-Y. (1987). Asymptotic properties maximum likelihood estimators likelihood ratio tests nonstandard conditions. Journal American Statistical Association, 82(398), 605–610.

Consider using constrained = TRUE. fit_nb <- estimate_hidden_pop(data = df, observed = ~m, auxiliary = ~n,                               reference_pop = ~N, method = "nb") #> Warning: Some alpha values < 0 (min = -1.244). Consider using constrained = TRUE.  # Test for overdispersion (Poisson vs NB) lrtest(fit_po, fit_nb) #> Likelihood ratio test #> ----------------------------------------  #> Model 1: POISSON   (logLik = -52.51 ) #> Model 2: NB   (logLik = -52.52 ) #> LR statistic: 0 on 1 df #> (Boundary-corrected: 0.5 * P(chi2 > LR), Self & Liang 1987) #> p-value: 0.5

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
text: data frame columns: group Group label derived alpha covariates, "()" alpha covariates specified. estimate Plug-estimate \(\hat{\xi}_g = \sum N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_g}\). estimate_bc Bias-corrected estimate \(\hat{\xi}^{BC}_g\). lower Lower bound (bias-corrected) confidence interval. upper Upper bound (bias-corrected) confidence interval. share_pct Group share percentage total \(\hat{\xi}\). multiple groups exist, attr(result, "total") contains total estimate, bias-corrected estimate, standard error, CI.

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
text: Point estimate. group \(g\) reference populations \(N_1, \ldots, N_n\) estimated exponent \(\hat{\alpha}_g\), plug-population size $$\hat{\xi}_g = \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_g}.$$ Bias correction via Taylor expansion. \(\xi\) nonlinear function \(\hat{\alpha}\), plug-estimate biased upward (Jensen's inequality, \(E[N^{\hat{\alpha}}] \geq N^{E[\hat{\alpha}]}\) \(N > 1\)). second-order Taylor expansion \(h(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^{\alpha}\) around \(\alpha_0\) gives approximate bias $$   \mathrm{Bias}(\hat{\xi}_g) \approx     \frac{1}{2} \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\alpha_g} (\log N_i)^2 \;     \mathbf{x}_g' \mathbf{V} \mathbf{x}_g, $$ \(\mathbf{x}_g\) design vector group \(g\) \(\mathbf{V}\) variance-covariance matrix \(\hat{\alpha}\). bias-corrected estimate \(\hat{\xi}^{BC}_g = \hat{\xi}_g - \widehat{\mathrm{Bias}}\). Model-based (homoscedastic) variance used bias correction rather HC-robust variance, HC3 can inflated high-leverage observations skewed data, leading overcorrection. constrained = TRUE, delta method accounts logit link: \(\mathrm{Var}(\alpha) = \mathrm{Var}(\eta) \cdot [\sigma'(\eta)]^2\) \(\sigma'(\eta) = \alpha(1-\alpha)\). Confidence intervals via monotone transformation. Wald interval first constructed link scale linear predictor: $$\hat{\eta}_g \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \mathrm{se}(\hat{\eta}_g),$$ standard error uses HC-robust variance. interval endpoints mapped monotone transformation \(g(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^{\alpha}\) (increasing \(N_i \geq 1\)) obtain \([\hat{\xi}_L, \hat{\xi}_U]\). constrained = TRUE, logit link applied exponentiation. Bias correction also applied CI bounds respective alpha values. Total across groups. multiple groups exist, total \(\hat{\xi} = \sum_g \hat{\xi}_g\) delta-method CI computed via gradient \(\nabla_\alpha \xi\) log-normal approximation positivity. stored attr(result, "total").

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
code: # Simulate synthetic data for 5 countries, 3 years each set.seed(42) n_obs <- 15 sim_data <- data.frame(   country = rep(paste0("C", 1:5), each = 3),   year    = rep(2018:2020, 5),   N       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 500000),   n       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 1000),   m       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 50) )  # Fit a Poisson model fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = sim_data, observed = ~m, auxiliary = ~n,   reference_pop = ~N, method = "poisson" ) #> Warning: Some alpha values < 0 (min = -0.467). Consider using constrained = TRUE.  # Population size with bias correction and 95% CI popsize(fit) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc          lower          upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174    -1977.66 -2.096793e-214 -1.865297e+220       100  # Without bias correction popsize(fit, bias_correction = FALSE) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc         lower         upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174  0.03262174 3.458684e-219 3.076829e+215       100  # 90% confidence interval popsize(fit, level = 0.90) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc          lower          upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174    -1977.66 -1.604466e-179 -2.437659e+185       100

title: Residuals for uncounted models — residuals.uncounted
text: Four residual types supported: Response residuals $$r_i = m_i - \hat\mu_i$$ Pearson residuals $$r_i = \frac{m_i - \hat\mu_i}{\sqrt{V(\hat\mu_i)}}$$ variance function \(V(\mu)\) depends model: Poisson: \(V(\mu) = \mu\) Negative Binomial: \(V(\mu) = \mu + \mu^2/\theta\) OLS/NLS: \(V(\mu) = \mu^2 \hat\sigma^2\) (delta-method approximation log scale) Deviance residuals Signed square root individual deviance contributions: $$r_i = \mathrm{sign}(m_i - \hat\mu_i)\,\sqrt{d_i}$$ \(d_i\) \(\)-th term deviance sum (see deviance.uncounted). OLS/NLS models, standardised log-scale residuals returned instead. Anscombe residuals Variance-stabilising residuals.
code: For Poisson (McCullagh & Nelder, 1989): \deqn{r_i = \frac{3\bigl(m_i^{2/3} - \hat\mu_i^{2/3}\bigr)}       {2\,\hat\mu_i^{1/6}}}  For Negative Binomial (Hilbe, 2011): \deqn{r_i = \frac{3\bigl(m_i^{2/3} - \hat\mu_i^{2/3}\bigr)}       {2\,\hat\mu_i^{1/6}\,(1 + \hat\mu_i/\theta)^{1/6}}}  For OLS/NLS: standardised log-scale residuals.

title: Residuals for uncounted models — residuals.uncounted
text: McCullagh, P. Nelder, J. . (1989). Generalized Linear Models, 2nd ed. Chapman & Hall. Hilbe, J. M. (2011). Negative Binomial Regression, 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. Pierce, D. . Schafer, D. W. (1986). Residuals generalized linear models. Journal American Statistical Association, 81(396), 977–986.

title: Rootogram for count model diagnostics — rootogram

Deviations zero indicate underfitting; zero, overfitting count. standing Observed bars stand x-axis fitted curve overlaid. Harder judge discrepancies hanging style eye must compare bar height curve. suspended Plots difference \(\sqrt{f_{\mathrm{obs}}} - \sqrt{f_{\mathrm{exp}}}\) directly. title: uncounted 0.6.0
text: Total CI printer: .print_popsize_table() now uses delta-method total CI attr(ps, "total") instead naively summing subgroup confidence bounds, ignored -group correlation. Constrained bias correction: second-order Taylor bias correction constrained models now includes logit curvature term alpha''(eta) = alpha(1-alpha)(1-2*alpha). Previously [alpha'(eta)]^2 used, producing incomplete bias correction alpha far 0.5. lrtest() nesting check: Now uses column-space inclusion (QR projection) detect non-nested model pairs, replacing previous heuristic. Also checks gamma specification compatibility.

title: uncounted 0.6.0
text: NB sandwich theta: Negative Binomial estimator now includes theta (dispersion) sandwich variance-covariance computation via dedicated NB covariance path using full per-observation score vector observed Hessian optim(hessian = TRUE). properly accounts theta estimation uncertainty coefficient standard errors. fitted object now includes theta_se, score_full, hessian_nll. Supports HC0, HC1, cluster-robust variance; HC2+ falls back HC1 message. CI workflows: Added GitHub Actions R-CMD-check (5 platforms), CRAN-like check, test coverage (Codecov), pkgdown site deployment. Unit test redesign: 340+ tests using shared 200-row simulated fixture, split CRAN (fast) extended (GitHub-) suites. Includes base R oracle tests glm(), lm(), sandwich::vcovHC(), MASS::glm.nb().

title: uncounted 0.6.0
text: Fixed references: replaced incorrect Zhang (2008) citation, added URLs, added Beresewicz & Pawlukiewicz (2020). OLS log(m+1) fitted-value scale limitation documented. Added URL BugReports fields DESCRIPTION. year column irregular_migration dataset converted factor.

title: uncounted 0.5.1
text: Added Aniela Czerniawska package author (original GitHub version). New dfbeta.uncounted() method leave-one-influence coefficients. Wrapper around loo() returning familiar n x p matrix coefficient changes. New dfpopsize() generic dfpopsize.uncounted() method leave-one-influence population size estimates. Returns named vector per-observation (per-country) changes total xi.

title: uncounted 0.5.0
text: vcov_type parameter estimate_hidden_pop() renamed vcov. now accepts either character string ("HC0" "HC5") function takes fitted object returns variance-covariance matrix (e.g., sandwich::vcovHC custom wrapper around sandwich::vcovCL()). countries parameter longer triggers cluster-robust standard errors. now used grouping popsize() loo(). Use new cluster parameter cluster-robust variance estimation.

title: uncounted 0.5.0
text: New cluster parameter estimate_hidden_pop() accepts one-sided formula (e.g., ~ country_code) request cluster-robust variance estimation via sandwich::vcovCL(). Full compatibility sandwich package new S3 methods: bread(), estfun(), hatvalues(), model.matrix(). allows direct use sandwich::vcovHC(), sandwich::vcovCL(), sandwich::sandwich() uncounted objects. New type = "working" option residuals() returns score residuals used internally sandwich variance computation. New update.uncounted() method enables re-fitting modified arguments (e.g., different weights vcov). Supports evaluate = FALSE return unevaluated call. New weights.uncounted() method returns observation weights fit. Compatibility fwb::vcovFWB() fractional weighted bootstrap variance estimation, thanks update() weights() methods. vcov parameter accepts function, enabling custom variance estimators:
code: # Cluster-robust via function fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(...,   vcov = function(x) sandwich::vcovCL(x, cluster = data$country))  # Observation-level via string (default) fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(..., vcov = "HC3")  # Cluster-robust via string + cluster formula fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(..., vcov = "HC1", cluster = ~ country_code)

title: uncounted 0.5.0
text: Fixed bug HC0 HC3 (HC types) produced identical standard errors clustering active. old implementation ignored vcov_type entirely cluster branch internal sandwich computation. Variance estimation now delegated sandwich package, correctly handles HC types observation-level cluster-robust cases. Fixed bug cluster-robust meat matrix computation extra μi\sqrt{\mu_i} factor incorrectly included Poisson NB score contributions. now resolved using sandwich::vcovCL().

title: uncounted 0.5.0
text: Variance-covariance computation now performed post-estimation using sandwich package rather internal .compute_sandwich_vcov() function. internal function retained estimator-level fallback vcov_model (used bias correction). estimator now returns model_matrix_full, bread_weights, score_residuals support bread() estfun() S3 methods. Added sandwich Imports DESCRIPTION. New file R/sandwich_methods.R containing bread.uncounted(), estfun.uncounted(), hatvalues.uncounted(), model.matrix.uncounted(). Expanded test suite 245 tests covering sandwich methods, cluster-robust variance, function-based vcov, HC0 != HC3 regression test.

title: uncounted 0.4.0
text: popsize() bootstrap_popsize() gain parameter stratified population size estimates (e.g., = ~ year, = ~ year + sex). Previously, stratification available covariate-varying alpha. New pseudo R2R^2 measures compare_models(): explained deviance (RD2R^2_D) Cameron-Windmeijer (RCW2R^2_{CW}) Poisson Negative Binomial models. Interactive Shiny application via run_app() five tabs: Data, Model Fitting, Population Size, Model Comparison, Diagnostics.

title: uncounted 0.4.0
text: Rootogram: white background bars hanging style, light-blue bars standing suspended styles clearer visual contrast. loo() call arguments now force-evaluated stored call object, fixing “object found” errors refitting within Shiny non-standard evaluation contexts. Shiny app: automatic conversion year-like columns factors prevent continuous covariate issues.

title: uncounted 0.4.0
text: Fixed Cameron-Windmeijer reference year: 1997 1996 (JBES 14(2), 209-220). Fixed non-ASCII characters country names (Cote d'Ivoire, Sao Tome e Principe) causing encoding issues platforms.

title: uncounted 0.3.1
text: Renamed internal sigmoid logit / inv_logit throughout package consistency standard statistical terminology.

title: uncounted 0.3.0
text: Added irregular_migration built-dataset (Poland 2019-2024) documentation. Added package vignettes: getting-started model-workflow. Full roxygen2 documentation exported functions.

title: uncounted 0.2.0
text: popsize() replaces xi() primary interface population size estimation. xi() retained deprecated alias. Bias-corrected population size estimates using second-order Taylor expansion (Jensen’s inequality correction). Delta-method confidence intervals total population size. profile_gamma() profiling gamma parameter grid. compare_models() side--side model comparison (AIC, BIC, log-likelihood). lrtest() likelihood-ratio tests nested models. loo() leave-one-diagnostics observation country. compare_loo() comparing LOO influence across models. Constrained estimation (constrained = TRUE): logit link α∈(0,1)\alpha \(0, 1) log link β>0\beta > 0. Fractional weighted bootstrap via bootstrap_popsize() using fwb package (Xu et al. 2020), cluster support. Observation weights via weights parameter. HC4, HC4m, HC5 robust standard error types. rootogram() assessing distributional fit (hanging, standing, suspended).

title: uncounted 0.2.0
text: Default gamma = "estimate" (NULL). Model-based (non-sandwich) variance stored vcov_model bias correction, separate HC-robust vcov. Improved numerical stability extreme parameter values. ~0 formula treated intercept-(empty model).

title: uncounted 0.1.0
text: Initial release. estimate_hidden_pop() fitting power-law model E(mi)=Niαi(γ+ni/Ni)βiE(m_i) = N_i^{\alpha_i} (\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta_i} using OLS, NLS, Poisson PML, Negative Binomial MLE. Covariate-varying α\alpha β\beta via formula interface (cov_alpha, cov_beta). Gamma parameter: estimated, fixed, excluded. HC0-HC3 robust standard errors. print() summary() S3 methods.
